subject: Physics
q: A particle of charge $1.6 \mu \mathrm{C}$ and mass $16 \mu \mathrm{~g}$ is present in a strong magnetic field of 6.28 T. The particle is then fired perpendicular to magnetic field. The time required for the particle to return to original location for the first time is ________ S. $(\pi=3.14)$
question: A particle of charge $1.6 \mu \mathrm{C}$ and mass $16 \mu \mathrm{~g}$ is present in a strong magnetic field of 6.28 T. The particle is then fired perpendicular to magnetic field. The time required for the particle to return to original location for the first time is ________ S. $(\pi=3.14)$
solution:
Angle between $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}$ of charge $\& \overrightarrow{\mathrm{~B}}$ is $90^{\circ}$ motion will be uniform circular motion time period is given by
$\mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \pi \mathrm{~m}}{\mathrm{qB}}=\frac{2 \pi \times 16 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~kg}}{1.6 \times 10^{-6} \times 6.28}$
$\mathrm{T}=0.01$ seconds
NTA Answer is 10
Correct Answer is 0 (nearest integer)

subject: Physics
q: A long wire carries a steady current. It is bent into a circle of one turn and the magnetic field at the centre of the coil is $B$. It is then bent into a circular loop of $n$ turns. The magnetic field at the centre of the coil will be
question: A long wire carries a steady current. It is bent into a circle of one turn and the magnetic field at the centre of the coil is $B$. It is then bent into a circular loop of $n$ turns. The magnetic field at the centre of the coil will be
solution: $B^{\prime}=\frac{\pi \mu_0 I}{2 r^{\prime}}=n^2 \frac{\mu_0 \pi \pi}{\ell}=n^2 B$.

The ratio of the magnetic fields due to the wire at distance <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>a</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> and <math><mn>2</mn><mi>a</mi></math> , respectively from the axis of the wire is:
question: A long, straight Wire of radius a carries a current distributed uniformly over its cross-section. The ratio of the magnetic fields due to the wire at distance <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>a</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> and <math><mn>2</mn><mi>a</mi></math> , respectively from the axis of the wire is:
solution:
BA=μ0ir2πa2=μ0ia32πa2=μ0iπa2a6=μ0i6πa
BB=μ0i2π2a
BABB=46=23

subject: Physics
q: Two long parallel wires are at a distance $2 \mathrm{~d}$ apart. They carry steady equal current flowing out of the plane of the paper as shown. The variation of the magnetic field along the line $\mathrm{XX}$ ' is given by
question: Two long parallel wires are at a distance $2 \mathrm{~d}$ apart. They carry steady equal current flowing out of the plane of the paper as shown. The variation of the magnetic field along the line $\mathrm{XX}$ ' is given by
solution:
The magnetic field in between because of each will be in opposite direction
$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text {in between }} & =\frac{\mu_0 i}{2 \pi x} \hat{j}-\frac{\mu_0 i}{2 \pi(2 d-x)}(-\hat{j}) \\
& =\frac{\mu_0 i}{2 \pi}\left[\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{2 d-x}\right](\hat{j})
\end{aligned}
$$
at $\quad x=d, \quad B_{\text {in between }}=0$
for $\quad x < d, \quad B_{\text {in between }}=(\hat{j})$
for $\quad x>d, \quad B_{\text {in between }}=(-\hat{j})$
towards $\mathrm{x}$ net magnetic field will add up and direction will be $(-\hat{\mathrm{j}})$
towards $\mathrm{x}^{\prime}$ net magnetic field will add up and direction will be $(\hat{\mathrm{j}})$

question: If an electron and a proton having same momenta enter perpendicular to a magnetic field, then

curved path of electron is more curved than that of the proton
path of proton is more curved

subject: Physics
q: An electron gun is placed inside a long solenoid of radius R on its axis. The solenoid has n turns/length and carries a current I. The electron gun shoots an electron along the radius of the solenoid with speed v. If the electron does not hit the surface of the solenoid, maximum possible value of v is (all symbols have their standard meaning):
question: An electron gun is placed inside a long solenoid of radius R on its axis. The solenoid has n turns/length and carries a current I. The electron gun shoots an electron along the radius of the solenoid with speed v. If the electron does not hit the surface of the solenoid, maximum possible value of v is (all symbols have their standard meaning):
solution:
Magnetic Field Inside Solenoid is B=μ0nI

Velocity ν has given to the electron 

So, Magnetic Force

F=qνB

as ν and B are perpendicular.

In a magnetic field, charge will move on a circular path. Here, If radius is just less than or equal to radius of solenoid then the electron does not hit the surface.

F=qνB=mν2R⇒mν=qBRas  q=eν=eBRmSo,V=eμ0nIRm

subject: Physics
q: A circular loop of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is carrying current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>i</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. The ratio of magnetic field at the centre of circular loop and at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> from the center of the loop on its axis is :
question: A circular loop of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is carrying current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>i</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. The ratio of magnetic field at the centre of circular loop and at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> from the center of the loop on its axis is :
solution:
The magnitude of magnetic field at the centre of loop is

B1 =Bcentre =μ∘i2R           …1

 

The magnitude of magnetic field at a point at a distance x from the centre along axis of loop is

Baxis =μ∘iR22R2+x232 

B2 =μ∘iR22232R3          …2      (since x=R)

Dividing equation 1 by equation 2, we get

B1B2=22:1

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I: The electric force changes the speed of the charged particle and hence changes its kinetic energy; whereas the magnetic force does not change the kinetic energy of the charged particle.
Statement II: The electric force accelerates the positively charged particle perpendicular to the direction of electric field. The magnetic force accelerates the moving charged particle along the direction of magnetic field.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I: The electric force changes the speed of the charged particle and hence changes its kinetic energy; whereas the magnetic force does not change the kinetic energy of the charged particle.
Statement II: The electric force accelerates the positively charged particle perpendicular to the direction of electric field. The magnetic force accelerates the moving charged particle along the direction of magnetic field.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
options: Both Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are correct.
Both Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are incorrect.

Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is correct but Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is incorrect.

Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is incorrect but Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is correct.
solution:
When a charged particle moves in an electric field, the field applies an electric force F→=qE→ along the field, so work done by electric field is non-zero resulting in accelerated motion, thus kinetic energy changes.

When a particle moves in a uniform magnetic field, the force due to magnetic field on it is perpendicular to its velocity, as force  F→=qv→×B→.
Since the magnetic field is constant, so force is constant. So, its direction of velocity constantly changes, but magnitude of velocity remains same. Hence, kinetic energy also does not change.

 Electric field accelerates the particle in the direction of field F→=qE→=ma→and magnetic field accelerates the particle perpendicular to the field F→=qv→×B→=ma→.

subject: Physics
q:
One of the two small circular coils, (none of them having any self - inductance) is suspended with a V-shaped copper wire, with plane horizontal. The other coil is placed just below the first one with plane horizontal. Both the coils are connected in series with a dc supply. The coils are found to attract each other with a force.
Which one of the following statements is incorrect?
question:
One of the two small circular coils, (none of them having any self - inductance) is suspended with a V-shaped copper wire, with plane horizontal. The other coil is placed just below the first one with plane horizontal. Both the coils are connected in series with a dc supply. The coils are found to attract each other with a force.
Which one of the following statements is incorrect?
options: Both the coils carry currents in the same direction.
Coils will attract each other, even if the supply is an ac source.
solution:
As the force between the two coils is attractive hence currents in the coil must be in the same direction and the force, $\mathrm{F} \propto \frac{\mathrm{I}_1 \mathrm{I}_2}{2 \pi \mathrm{d}}$
where $I_1$ and $I_2$ are the currents flowing through the coils.

question: An insulating thin rod of length <math><mi>l</mi></math> has a linear charge density <math><mi>ρ</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi>ρ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mfrac><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow></mfrac></math> on it. The rod is rotated about an axis passing through the origin <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and perpendicular to the rod. If the rod makes <math><mi>n</mi></math> rotations per second, then the time averaged magnetic moment of the rod is:
solution:
ω=2πn rad s-1



dQ=ρ dx

=ρ0lxdx

Current due to elementary charge dQ

dI=dQ ω2π 

Magnetic dipole moment

dM=dI×A

∫dM=∫0lω2π×ρ0l×xdx πx2

M=π nρ0l34

question: A beam of protons with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> enters a uniform magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> to the magnetic field, the pitch of the resulting helical path of protons is close to : (Mass of the proton<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>67</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>27</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>, charge of the proton<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>69</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>19</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>)
solution:
Pitch

=(Vcosθ)T

=(Vcosθ)2πmeB

=4×105cos60°2π0.3×101.67×10-271.69×1019

4 cm

question: One of the two identical conducting wires of length <math><mi>L</mi></math> is bent in the form of a circular loop and the other one into a circular coil of <math><mi>N</mi></math> identical turns. q: A metal surface is illuminated by a radiation of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4500</mn><mover><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mo>∘</mo></mover></math>. The ejected photo-electron enters a constant magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mT</mi></math> making an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the magnetic field. If it starts revolving in a circular path of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>, the work function of the metal is approximately
question: A metal surface is illuminated by a radiation of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4500</mn><mover><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mo>∘</mo></mover></math>. The ejected photo-electron enters a constant magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mT</mi></math> making an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the magnetic field. If it starts revolving in a circular path of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>, the work function of the metal is approximately
solution:
From the Einstein's photoelectric equation K=hcλ-ϕ⇒ϕ=hcλ-K

Now R=mvBq⇒v=RBqm⇒K=R2B2q22m

Therefore, ϕ=hcλ-R2B2q22m=6.63×10-34×3×1084500×10-10-2×10-3×2×10-3×1.6×10-1922×9.1×10-31⇒ϕ=4.42-2.25×10-19 J=2.271.6 eV=1.36 eV

q:
A particle of charge $16 \times 10^{-16} \mathrm{C}$ moving with velocity $10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ along $x$-axis enters a region where magnetic field of induction
$\vec{B}$ is along the $y$-axis and an electric field of magnitude $10^4 \mathrm{Vm}^{-1}$ is along the negative $z$-axis.
If the charged particle continues moving along $x$-axis, the magnitude of $\vec{B}$ is :
question:
A particle of charge $16 \times 10^{-16} \mathrm{C}$ moving with velocity $10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ along $x$-axis enters a region where magnetic field of induction
$\vec{B}$ is along the $y$-axis and an electric field of magnitude $10^4 \mathrm{Vm}^{-1}$ is along the negative $z$-axis.
If the charged particle continues moving along $x$-axis, the magnitude of $\vec{B}$ is :
solution:
Since particle is moving undeflected
So, $q_E=q v B$
$$
\Rightarrow B=\frac{E}{V}=\frac{10^4}{10}=10^3 \mathrm{wb} / \mathrm{m}^2
$$

subject: Physics
q: A short bar magnet placed with its axis at $30^{\circ}$ with an external field of 800 Gauss, experiences a torque of $0.016 \mathrm{~N}. \mathrm{m}$. The work done in moving it from most stable to most unstable position is $\alpha \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~J}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: A short bar magnet placed with its axis at $30^{\circ}$ with an external field of 800 Gauss, experiences a torque of $0.016 \mathrm{~N}. \mathrm{m}$. The work done in moving it from most stable to most unstable position is $\alpha \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~J}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
For a magnetic dipole in an external field, torque is $\tau = mB\sin\theta$
At 30°: $0.016 = m \times 0.08 \times \sin 30°$
$0.016 = m \times 0.08 \times 0.5$
$m = 0.4$ A·m$^2$
Work done from most stable (aligned, 0°) to most unstable (anti-aligned, 180°) position equals the change in potential energy:
$W = U_0° - U_{180°} = -mB\cos 0° - (-mB\cos 180°) = -mB - mB = 2mB$
$W = 2 \times 0.4 \times 0.08 = 0.064$ J $= 64 \times 10^{-3}$ J
Therefore, $\alpha = 64$

subject: Physics
q:
An electron is moving along the positive x-axis. If uniform magnetic field is applied parallel to the negative z-axis, then

A.The electron will experience magnetic force along positive y-axis

B.The electron will experience magnetic force along negative y-axis

C.The electron will not experience any force in magnetic field

D.The electron will continue to move along the positive x-axis

E.The electron will move along circular path in magnetic field

Choose the correct answer from the option given below:
question:
An electron is moving along the positive x-axis. If uniform magnetic field is applied parallel to the negative z-axis, then

A.The electron will experience magnetic force along positive y-axis

B.The electron will experience magnetic force along negative y-axis

C.The electron will not experience any force in magnetic field

D.The electron will continue to move along the positive x-axis

E.The electron will move along circular path in magnetic field

Choose the correct answer from the option given below:
solution:
The Lorentz force is given by

F→=qv→×B→

=-ev→×B→

So, F^=i^×k^=-j^.

F→ is  along -y axis 

As the magnetic field is perpendicular to the velocity, motion would be circular.

subject: Physics
q: Two circular coils P and Q of 100 turns each have same radius of π cm. The currents in P and R are 1 A and 2 A respectively. P and Q are placed with their planes mutually perpendicular with their centers coincide. The resultant magnetic field induction at the center of the coils is x mT, where x= ______. [Use μ0=4π×10−7 T m A−1]
question: Two circular coils P and Q of 100 turns each have same radius of π cm. The currents in P and R are 1 A and 2 A respectively. P and Q are placed with their planes mutually perpendicular with their centers coincide. The resultant magnetic field induction at the center of the coils is x mT, where x= ______. [Use μ0=4π×10−7 T m A−1]
solution:
Consider two coils as shown below:

Magnetic field due to P, BP=Nμ0i12r=100×μ0×12π×10-2=2×10−3 T

Magnetic field due to Q, 

BQ=Nμ0i22r=100×μ0×22π×10-2=4×10−3 T

Therefore, Bnet=BP2+BQ2

=20  mT

Hence, x=20

subject: Physics
q: An infinitely long straight wire carrying current $I$ is bent in a planer shape as shown in the diagram. The radius of the circular part is $r$. The magnetic field at the centre $O$ of the circular loop is :
question: An infinitely long straight wire carrying current $I$ is bent in a planer shape as shown in the diagram. The radius of the circular part is $r$. The magnetic field at the centre $O$ of the circular loop is :
solution:
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}_0=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}_{\mathrm{AB}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}_{\mathrm{DE}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}_{\mathrm{BCD}}$
$=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{i}}{4 \pi \mathrm{r}} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{i}}{4 \pi \mathrm{r}} \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{i}}{2 \mathrm{r}} \hat{\mathrm{i}}$
$=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{i}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}} \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{i}}{2 \mathrm{r}} \hat{\mathrm{i}}$
$=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{i}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}(1-\pi) \hat{\mathrm{i}}$
$=-\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{i}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}(\pi-1) \hat{\mathrm{i}}$

subject: Physics
q: A proton and a deutron ( $q=+\mathrm{e}, m=2.0 \mathrm{u})$ having same kinetic energies enter a region of uniform magnetic field $\vec{B}$, moving perpendicular to $\vec{B}$. The ratio of the radius $r_d$ of deutron path to the radius $r_p$ of the proton path is:
question: A proton and a deutron ( $q=+\mathrm{e}, m=2.0 \mathrm{u})$ having same kinetic energies enter a region of uniform magnetic field $\vec{B}$, moving perpendicular to $\vec{B}$. The ratio of the radius $r_d$ of deutron path to the radius $r_p$ of the proton path is:
solution:
In uniform magnetic field,
$\mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{m} \nu}{\mathrm{qB}}=\frac{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~m}(\mathrm{~K} . \mathrm{E})}}{\mathrm{qB}}$
Since same K.E
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{R} \propto \frac{\sqrt{\mathrm{m}}}{\mathrm{q}} \\& \therefore \frac{\mathrm{R}_{\text {deutron }}}{\mathrm{R}_{\text {proton }}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{d}}}{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{p}}}} \times \frac{\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{d}}} \\& =\sqrt{2} \times 1 \\& \therefore \gamma_{\mathrm{d}}: \gamma_{\mathrm{p}}=\sqrt{2}: 1\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
A coil is placed in magnetic field such that plane of coil is perpendicular to the direction of magnetic field. The magnetic flux through a coil can be changed:

A. By changing the magnitude of the magnetic field within the coil.

B. By changing the area of coil within the magnetic field.

C. By changing the angle between the direction of magnetic field and the plane of the coil.

D. By reversing the magnetic field direction abruptly without changing its magnitude.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
A coil is placed in magnetic field such that plane of coil is perpendicular to the direction of magnetic field. The magnetic flux through a coil can be changed:

A. By changing the magnitude of the magnetic field within the coil.

B. By changing the area of coil within the magnetic field.

C. By changing the angle between the direction of magnetic field and the plane of the coil.

D. By reversing the magnetic field direction abruptly without changing its magnitude.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
solution:
Magnetic flux through a coil is given by ϕ=B→.A→=BAcosθ, where,Bis magnetic field, A is area of coil and θ is the angle between B and A.

Thus, from above expression, we can say that magnetic flux can be changed by changing magnetic field, area of coil, change the angle between B and A.

Hence, correct options are A, B and C.

subject: Physics
q: The magnetic force acting on charged particle of charge $2 \mu \mathrm{C}$ in magnetic field of $2 T$ acting in $y-$ direction, when the particle velocity is $(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}) \times 10^6 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ is
question: The magnetic force acting on charged particle of charge $2 \mu \mathrm{C}$ in magnetic field of $2 T$ acting in $y-$ direction, when the particle velocity is $(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}) \times 10^6 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ is
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { } \vec{F}=q(\vec{V} \times \vec{B}) \\
& =2 \times 10^{-6}\left[(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}) \times 10^6 \times 2 \hat{j}\right] \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$

$=2 \times 4 \hat{k}=8 N$ in Z-direction.

subject: Physics
q: A uniform conducting wire of length is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo></math> and resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is wound up as a current carrying coil in the shape of an equilateral triangle of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> and then in the form of a square of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math>. The coil is connected to a voltage source <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>.</mo></math> The ratio of magnetic moment of the coils in case of equilateral triangle to that for square is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><msqrt><mi>y</mi></msqrt></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math> is ____________.
question: A uniform conducting wire of length is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo></math> and resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is wound up as a current carrying coil in the shape of an equilateral triangle of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> and then in the form of a square of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math>. The coil is connected to a voltage source <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>.</mo></math> The ratio of magnetic moment of the coils in case of equilateral triangle to that for square is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><msqrt><mi>y</mi></msqrt></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math> is ____________.
solution:
4an1=24a

thus n1=6 turns

3an2=24a⇒n2=8 turns

M1M2=n1iA1n2iA2=6×a28×a2×34=31

M2M1=13=1y

y=3

subject: Physics
q:
Three long straight wires carrying current are arranged mutually parallel as shown in the figure. The force experienced by 15 cm length of wire $Q$ is $\_\_\_\_$.

($\mu_{\mathrm{o}}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~T}. \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{A}$)
question:
Three long straight wires carrying current are arranged mutually parallel as shown in the figure. The force experienced by 15 cm length of wire $Q$ is $\_\_\_\_$.

($\mu_{\mathrm{o}}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~T}. \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{A}$)
solution:
The force per unit length between parallel wires is $F/L = \frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2\pi d}$.
Force on Q from P (opposite currents, repulsive towards R): $\frac{F_{QP}}{L} = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 3 \times 1}{2\pi \times 0.03} = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ N/m.
Force on Q from R (same currents, attractive towards R): $\frac{F_{QR}}{L} = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1 \times 2}{2\pi \times 0.02} = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ N/m.
Total force per unit length towards R: $4 \times 10^{-5}$ N/m.
For 0.15 m length: $F = 4 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.15 = 6 \times 10^{-6}$ N towards R.

question: Figure A and B shown two long straight wires of circular cross-section ( a and b with a<b ), carrying current I which is uniformly distributed across the cross-section. The magnitude of magnetic field B varies with radius r and can be represented as:

subject: Physics
q: For a moving coil galvanometer, the deflection in the coil is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>05</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi></math> when a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi></math> is passed through it. If the torsional constant of suspension wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>rad</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the magnetic field is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> and the number of turns in the coil is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn></math>, the area of each turn (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> ) is :
question: For a moving coil galvanometer, the deflection in the coil is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>05</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi></math> when a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi></math> is passed through it. If the torsional constant of suspension wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>rad</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the magnetic field is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> and the number of turns in the coil is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn></math>, the area of each turn (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> ) is :
solution:
Given: θ=0.05 rad, I=10 mA, K=4×10-5 N m rad-1, B=0.01 T, N=200

The torsional constant of a wire used in a moving coil galvanometer is given by 

K=NIABθ, where, A is area of each turn

So, A=KθNIB=4×10-5×0.05200×10×10-3×0.01

Thus, A=10-4 m2=1 cm2

subject: Physics
q: A Helmholtz coil has pair of loops, each with $N$ turns and radius $R$. They are placed coaxially at distance $R$ and the same current $I$ flows through the loops in the same direction. The magnitude of magnetic field at $P$, midway between the centres $A$ and $C$, is given by (Refer to figure):
question: A Helmholtz coil has pair of loops, each with $N$ turns and radius $R$. They are placed coaxially at distance $R$ and the same current $I$ flows through the loops in the same direction. The magnitude of magnetic field at $P$, midway between the centres $A$ and $C$, is given by (Refer to figure):
solution:
Point $P$ is situated at the mid-point of the line joining the centres of the circular wires which have same radii (R). The magnetic fields $(\vec{B})$ at $\mathrm{P}$ due to the currents in the wires are in same direction.
Magnitude of magnetic field at point, $P$
$B=2\left\{\frac{\mu_0 N I R^2}{2\left(R^2+\frac{R^2}{4}\right)^{3 / 2}}\right\}$
$=\frac{\mu_0 N I R^2}{\frac{5^{3 / 2}}{8}}=\frac{8 \mu_0 N I}{5^{3 / 2} R}$

question: Four identical long solenoids A, B, C and D are connected to each other as shown in the figure. If the magnetic field at the center of A is 3 T the field at the center of C would be : (Assume that the magnetic field is confined with in the volume of respective solenoid).
solution: ϕ∝i⇒B∝i, so, the field at the center of C=33=1 T.

subject: Physics
q:
The current passing through a conducting loop in the form of equilateral triangle of side $4 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}$ is 2 A. The magnetic field at its centroid is $\alpha \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~T}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
(Given : $\mu_{\mathrm{o}}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7}$ SI units)
question:
The current passing through a conducting loop in the form of equilateral triangle of side $4 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}$ is 2 A. The magnetic field at its centroid is $\alpha \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~T}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
(Given : $\mu_{\mathrm{o}}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7}$ SI units)
solution:
For an equilateral triangle of side $a$, the distance of the centroid from any side is $r = \frac{a}{2\sqrt{3}}$.
Given side $a = 4\sqrt{3}$ cm $= 4\sqrt{3} \times 10^{-2}$ m.
So, $r = \frac{4\sqrt{3} \times 10^{-2}}{2\sqrt{3}} = 2 \times 10^{-2}$ m.
The magnetic field at the centroid due to one side of the triangle is $B_1 = \frac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi r} (\sin \theta_1 + \sin \theta_2)$.
For an equilateral triangle, $\theta_1 = \theta_2 = 60^\circ$.
$B_1 = \frac{10^{-7} \times 2}{2 \times 10^{-2}} (\sin 60^\circ + \sin 60^\circ) = 10^{-5} \times (\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}) = \sqrt{3} \times 10^{-5}$ T.
The total magnetic field at the centroid due to all three sides is $B = 3B_1 = 3\sqrt{3} \times 10^{-5}$ T.
Comparing with $B = \alpha \times 10^{-5}$ T, we get $\alpha = 3\sqrt{3}$.

subject: Physics
q: A magnetic dipole in a uniform magnetic field has: (Take zero potential energy when magnetic dipole is perpendicular to magnetic field)
question: A magnetic dipole in a uniform magnetic field has: (Take zero potential energy when magnetic dipole is perpendicular to magnetic field)
options: Maximum potential energy when the torque is maximum
Zero potential energy when the torque is maximum
Zero potential energy when the torque is minimum
Minimum potential energy when the torque is maximum
solution:
Torque, τ=MBsinθ

and potential energy, U=-MBcosθ

τ is maximum when θ=90o

U=-MBcos90o=0.

subject: Physics
q: As shown in the figure, a long straight conductor with semicircular arc of radius π10 m is carrying current I = 3A. The magnitude of the magnetic field. at the center O of the arc is: (The permeability of the vacuum = 4π×10-7NA-2 )
question: As shown in the figure, a long straight conductor with semicircular arc of radius π10 m is carrying current I = 3A. The magnitude of the magnetic field. at the center O of the arc is: (The permeability of the vacuum = 4π×10-7NA-2 )
solution:
The formula to calculate the magnetic field BC produced at the centre of a circular current carrying conductor is given by

BC= μoI4πrθ.......................(1)

where, μ0 is the permeability of free space, I is the current through the conductor, r is the radius of the circular conductor and θ is the angle subtended by the arc at the centre.

From the given figure, it is clear that the arc of the conductor makes an angle π at its centre.

Substitute the known values of the parameters into equation (1) to calculate the required magnetic field.

BC=4π×10-7 N A-2×3 A4×π×π10 m×π= 3×10-6 T×106 μT1 T= 3 μT

subject: Physics
q: A charged particle going around in a circle can be considered to be a current loop. A particle of a mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> carrying charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi></math> is moving in a plane with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math> under the influence of magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math>. The magnetic moment of this moving particle is :
question: A charged particle going around in a circle can be considered to be a current loop. A particle of a mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> carrying charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi></math> is moving in a plane with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math> under the influence of magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math>. The magnetic moment of this moving particle is :
solution:
i=qT=qv2πr

M=i×πr2

=2v2πr×πr2

=12qv×mvqB

Direction of M→ is opposite of B→ therefore

M→=-mv2B→2B2

subject: Physics
q: N equally spaced charges each of value q , are placed on a circle of radius R . The circle rotates about its axis with an angular velocity $\omega$ as shown in the figure. A bigger Amperian loop B encloses the whole circle where as a smaller Amperian loop A encloses a small segment. The difference between enclosed currents, $I_A-I_B$, for the given Amperian loops is
question: N equally spaced charges each of value q , are placed on a circle of radius R . The circle rotates about its axis with an angular velocity $\omega$ as shown in the figure. A bigger Amperian loop B encloses the whole circle where as a smaller Amperian loop A encloses a small segment. The difference between enclosed currents, $I_A-I_B$, for the given Amperian loops is
solution: $\begin{aligned} & I_A=\frac{N q}{\frac{2 \pi}{\omega}} \\ & I_A=\frac{N q \omega}{2 \pi}, I_B=0 \\ & I_A=I_B=\frac{N q \omega}{2 \pi}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A proton is moving undeflected in a region of crossed electric and magnetic fields at a constant speed of $2 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. When the electric field is switched off, the proton moves along a circular path of radius 2 cm . The magnitude of electric field is $x \times 10^4 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C}$. The value of $x$ is _______ Take the mass of the proton $=1.6 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}$.
question: A proton is moving undeflected in a region of crossed electric and magnetic fields at a constant speed of $2 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. When the electric field is switched off, the proton moves along a circular path of radius 2 cm . The magnitude of electric field is $x \times 10^4 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C}$. The value of $x$ is _______ Take the mass of the proton $=1.6 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}$.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & B v q=E q \\ & E=B v \\ & r=\frac{v m}{B q} \\ & B=\frac{m v}{r q} \\ & E=\left(\frac{m v}{r q}\right) v=\frac{m v^2}{r q} \\ & =\frac{1.6 \times 10^{-27} \times 4 \times 10^{10}}{2 \times 10^{-2} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \\ & =2 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C} \\ & x=2\end{aligned}$
